coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of carbon nanodots with surface state-modulated fluorescence for highly sensitive and real-time detection of water in organic solvents
coredata.dc:creator: Ye, Chengliang
Qin, Yujuan
Huang, Pengcheng
Chen, Anfeng
Wu, Fang-Ying
coredata.dc:description: In our study, the carbon nanodots (CDs) were synthesized by one-step solvothermal method using resorcinol as the only presusor. The obtained CDs contained abundant unsaturated oxygen-containing groups resulting from the surface oxidation. A novel, simple, and real-time fluorescent assay for the detection of water in various organic solvents was thus established by reducing the surface oxidation states. Excellent reversibility can be readily achieved by the external stimulus water and N,N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC). The water-induced sensitive (limit of detection = 0.006%, v/v, in ethanol) and ultrafast (<1 s) response in emission properties was capable of water determination in spirit samples in both solution and solid-state paper test strips.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Resorcinol
Water content
Spirit samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of quantum yield (QY) of the CDs
Fluorescence assay for water content in organic solvents
Quantitative determination and qualitative visualization of water content in real samples
Preparation and characterization of the CDs
Fluorescence detection of water using the CDs
Mechanism for the fluorescence response of the CDs to H2O
Fluorescence detection of water content in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and (B) size distribution of the as-prepared CDs. The inset in (A) is the HRTEM image.
(A) Raman spectrum of the CDs. (B) XPS survey scan of the CDs, and high resolution spectra of (C) O 1s and (D) C 1s peaks.

(A) UV-vis absorption spectra of resorcinol and the CDs solution. (B) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs at different excitation wavelengths (380–500 nm). Inset: the photograph of the CDs solutions under daylight (left) and a 365 nm UV lamp (right).
(A) Reaction time profile of the CDs for the detection of H2O. (B) The effect of the CDs concentration on the detection of H2O.

Fluorescence spectra of the CDs (5.1 mg mL−1) in ethanol responding to different water contents (0–10%, v/v). Inset: the photograph of the CDs in ethanol with different water contents under a 365 nm UV lamp. (B) The change of the fluorescence intensity of the CDs at 504 nm (ΔF) corresponding to (A). Inset: the fitting curve between ΔF and water content within the range of 0–1% (v/v).

(A) Fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F0) of the CDs (5.1 mg mL−1) in the presence and absence of different organic solvents (10%, v/v). (B) Reversibility of the CDs in ethanol by introducing equivalent mole of H2O and DCC.

(A) XPS survey scan of the CDs in the presence of water, and high resolution spectra of (B) O 1s and (C) C 1s peaks. (D) Fluorescence decay curve of the CDs in the presence and absence of H2O (λex = 400 nm).
Fluorescence photograph of the CDs-impregnated test strips for spirit samples with various water contents.
Schematic illustration of fluorescence detection of water in organic solvents by altering the surface states of the CDs.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of carbon nanodots with surface state-modulated fluorescence for highly sensitive and real-time detection of water in organic solvents

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In our study, the carbon nanodots (CDs) were synthesized by one-step solvothermal method using resorcinol as the only presusor. The obtained CDs contained abundant unsaturated oxygen-containing groups resulting from the surface oxidation. A novel, simple, and real-time fluorescent assay for the detection of water in various organic solvents was thus established by reducing the surface oxidation states. Excellent reversibility can be readily achieved by the external stimulus water and N,N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC). The water-induced sensitive (limit of detection = 0.006%, v/v, in ethanol) and ultrafast (<1 s) response in emission properties was capable of water determination in spirit samples in both solution and solid-state paper test strips.

ce:section.ce:para: The presence of water can adversely affect many chemical reactions by impeding not only the yield of chemicals but also their activities and uses. Besides, the control of its content in organic solvents is essential for many practical applications such as environmental monitoring, food processing and pharmaceutical manufacturing, in which water is generally identified as a common contaminant and impurity [1]. Therefore, it is very important to establish a simple, fast, and reliable analytical strategy for accurate quantification of water in organic solvents not only in synthetic laboratories but also for industrial applications.

Generally, Karl Fischer titration is the most broadly used method for the detection of water [2–4]. However, such approach requires specialized instruments, well-trained personnel and relatively lengthy assays. Moreover, the use of toxic and expensive chemical reagents, undesirable side reactions and interferences from other common co-existing species are usually encountered [5]. These disadvantages seriously prevent it from being a universal method. Fluorescence-based systems for water detection have attracted much attention as the alternatives to Karl Fischer titration because of their significant advantages involving operational simplicity, high sensitivity, low cost and rapid response as well as their capability of remote and in situ monitoring [6]. To date such water sensors are mainly restricted to organic molecules [6–17]. Despite great successes, many of them failed to detect water content at very low levels (<1% v/v) and cannot be readily recovered and reused. In addition, they are significantly influenced by environmental factors such as the change of polarity or pH of the sensing media [8,18,19], and only applied in a very limited number of specific organic solvents depending on their solubility.

Recently fluorescent nanomaterials have gained increasing importance in achieving water detection benefitting from rapid analysis, easy miniaturization and facile recovery [20–31]. Although each of these methods possesses their own features, most are based on the effect of O−H oscillators or water-induced aggregation/disaggregation mechanism [23,24,26–31]. Therefore, it is still very critical to develop novel materials using unusual sensing mechanism for robust detection of water in organic solvents more simply, selectively and sensitively. Carbon nanodots (CDs) as an emerging fascinating carbon nanomaterial have attracted rapidly growing interest in recent years [32]. Owing to their excellent water dispersibility, tunable optical property, chemical- and photostability, low cost and good biocompatibility, the CDs have been used as potential candidates in numerous applications including bioimaging [33], catalysis [34], optoelectronics [35], and particularly sensing [36,37], due to the unique fluorescent properties caused by the quantum confinement and surface effects.

Herein, we reported a simple approach for the preparation of cost-effective and highly photoluminescent CDs by using the solvothermal treatment of resorcinol via one-pot synthesis. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the as-prepared CDs emitted strong yellow-green fluorescence in organic solvents, and the unsaturated oxygen-containing groups (e.g., carbonyl groups) emerged on the surfaces of the CDs mainly resulting from the oxidation of phenolic hydroxyl groups in resorcinol. When trace amount of water is present in organic solution of the CDs, it would unexpectedly cause strong fluorescence quenching by changing the CDs surface state through the hydration of the carbonyl groups to form a gem-diol species, thus enabling the CDs to be directly exploited as the label-free fluorescent sensing platform for the detection of water. Interestingly, by subsequently introducing N,N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), a dehydration agent, the turn-off fluorescence was greatly recovered due to the removal of water molecule from the surfaces. We also demonstrated that the CDs can serve as a general agent for the detection of water in a variety of organic solvents such as methanol, DMF, acetonitrile, and DMSO. Finally we successfully achieved visual detection of water content in spirit samples with the CDs-coated test strips.

Resorcinol, pyrocatechol, hydroquinone, N,N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), Quinine sulfate were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dialysis bag (retained molecular weight 3500 Da) was obtained from Shanghai Green bird Science & Technology Development Co. (Shanghai, China). Dimethyl formamide (DMF), methanol (MeOH), acetone, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), dichloromethane (DCM), toluene, methanol, trichloromethane (CHCl3), petroleum ether (PTE), carbon tetrachloride (CCl4), ethylene glycol (EG), acetonitrile (ACN), ethyl acetate (EA), isopropanol (IPA), n-hexane and all other chemicals (analytical-reagent grade) were acquired from Shanghai Qinxi Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). In order to rule out the effect from the minimal amount of water in organic solvents, the solvents were dried before use. For example, to obtain anhydrous DMSO, the CaH2 powder was introduced to the commercial DMSO under vigorous stirring for 12 h, and then the solvent was evaporated under reduced pressure purged with nitrogen. Finally, 4 Å molecular sieves were added for long-term use. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm−1) was used throughout the experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted on a JEOL-model 2100 F instrument with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fluorescence spectra are recorded on an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with a slit width of excitation set at 2.5 nm and emission set at 5.0 nm. UV-vis absorption spectra are obtained on a Shimadzu-2550 UV-vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) using a 1.0 cm quartz cell. Infrared spectra were taken with KBr pellets on a Nicolet 5700 FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet, America). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were measured by ESCALAB250Xi photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Raman spectrum was recorded using Horiba-Jobin, Lab Ram HR spectrometer. The as-prepared samples were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Panalytical) utilizing Cu Ka radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) at 40 kV and 30 mA. The zeta potential measurement was carried out at room temperature (90Plus PALS, PRODUCTS).

Typically, resorcinol (0.2315 g) was added into anhydrous ethanol (30 mL), and then the solution was transferred into a 50 mL Telfon-equipped stainless-stell autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 7 h. After cooling to room temperature, the color of the solution changed from initial colorless to brown indicating the formation of CDs. The resultant transparent brown solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane and further dialyzed against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane for 24 h to remove the excess reactants. The CDs solid was finally obtained after centrifugation (12, 000 rpm, 15 min) and placed in vacuum oven at 45 °C and heated for 24 h to remove the residual small molecules. The obtained solid product is redispersed in ethanol and stored at 4 °C for further use.

To prepare the CDs with high quantum yields, the synthesis conditions were varied and optimized, during which the synthesis temperature was changed from 120 to 200 °C, and the reaction time was varied from 4 to 10 h, and water and ethanol were selected as the solvent, respectively. Additionally, the two isomers of resorcinol, pyrocatechol and hydroquinone, were used as precursors respectively, under the same procedures described above for resorcinol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of quantum yield (QY) of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Aqueous quinine sulfate solution in 0.05 M sulfuric acid (QY = 0.56) was chosen as a standard for the QY of the CDs. QY values were determined according to eq (1):
(1) QYu = QYs*Fu/Fs*As/Au*R2 u/R2 s
Where QY represents quantum yield for the unknown (QYu) and standard (QYs), and F represents the measured integrated fluorescence intensities of the unknown (Fu) and the standard (Fs). Absorbance readings (A) of the unknown (Au) and the standard (As) were values collected at 400 nm. R is the refractive index of the unknown (Ru) and standard (Rs). In this study the Ru and Rs values were equal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay for water content in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, the original solution of the CDs (5.1 mg mL−1) was prepared by dissolving the CDs solid into anhydrous ethanol. 100 μL of the mixture of water/ethanol with different percentages of water were then separately added into 900 μL of CDs to make a final water concentrations ranging from 0 to 10% (v/v). The resulting mixtures were mixed well, and then the fluorescence spectra were recorded (λex = 400 nm). The same procedure was also performed for detection of water in DMF, ACN, or DMSO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative determination and qualitative visualization of water content in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
Several kinds of spirit samples (Erguotou, Zhijiang wine, Site wine, Jing wine, Hanlong wine, Millet wine) were obtained from the local supermarket. First, the samples were filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove the larger bulk particles. Second, a certain volume of each sample was diluted with anhydrous ethanol and sonicated for 20 min to dissolve completely. Each treated sample solution (100 μL) was mixed with CDs solution (900 μL) to ensure that the water content was between 0 and 1% (v/v). Last, fluorescence spectra were obtained. Based on the calibration curve obtained in Fig. 5 B
and the recorded fluorescence intensity at 504 nm, the water content in each sample was calculated and compared to those obtained by the mark.

The test strips were fabricated with common filter paper. They were firstly immersed into the ethanol solution of the CDs and incubated overnight. Next, the strips were removed from the solution and dried naturally. After drying, the testing samples were separately dripped onto the test strips to judge the water content rapidly by the naked eyes with observable change of fluorescence intensity excited by a 365 nm UV lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent CDs were obtained by solvothermal treatment of resorcinol. To obtain the CDs with highest possible fluorescence intensity, we systematically studied the effects of different solvents, raw material concentration, reaction time and solvothermal temperature. It was found that the CDs synthesized with ethanol as the reaction solvent emitted much stronger fluorescence than with water (Fig. S1A). In addition, it was revealed that as the concentration of resorcinol increased, the fluorescence intensity displayed a maximum value at 0.07 mol L−1. Similarly, the fluorescence intensity reached highest at 200 °C and under the reaction time of 7 h (Fig. S1B-D). All subsequent experiments were thus carried out under these optimal conditions. Moreover, a control experiment was also performed using pyrocatechol or hydroquinone as the only precursor under optimal synthetic procedures for resorcinol. It was demonstrated that the CDs synthesized by resorcinol had a much higher quantum yield (6.9%) than those synthesized by two other carbon sources (0.47% and 0.95%, respectively), which could be presumably due to their different modulation effect on the chemical and electronic characteristics of the CDs [38].

The morphology and size of the CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). It depicted that CDs are spherical and well monodispersed (Fig. 1A). As estimated from the TEM image, the diameters of the CDs were mainly distributed in the narrow range of 2–4 nm with an average size of 2.8 nm (Fig. 1B). The HRTEM image of the CDs (inset in Fig. 1A) indicated high crystallinity with a lattice parameter of 0.242 nm corresponding to the (1120) lattice fringe of graphene [39]. The Raman spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 2A) showed the characteristic D band at 1349 cm−1 and G band at 1586 cm−1, which were related to the disordered C atoms at the edges of the CDs (sp3 carbon) and sp2-bond C atoms, respectively. The intensity ratio ID/IG was 0.67, indicating good crystalline nature of the CDs [40]. The XRD pattern (Fig. S2) revealed a broad diffraction peak at about 25° (2θ), which confirmed the existence of highly disordered amorphous carbon in the CDs [38].

The XPS spectrum was determined to probe the elemental composition and surface groups of the CDs. The XPS survey spectrum of CDs showed a C 1s peak at ca. 292.1 eV, along with an O 1s peak at ca. 538.4 eV (Fig. 2B). The O/C atomic ratio was as high as 0.25 (the O/C atomic ratio is usually 0.05–0.4) [39], indicating the presence of abundant oxygen-containing functional groups in the CDs [39,40]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 2C) can be deconvoluted into two different peaks, which corresponded to the signals of C=O (532.2 eV) and C-OH/C-O-C (533.4 eV). Fig. 2D showed the well-fitted C 1s spectrum which can be divided into three different peaks. The peaks located at 285.0, 287.0 and 288.5 eV were attributed to the signals of C-C/C=C, C-O and C=O, respectively [39]. The surface functional groups of the CDs were further confirmed by FT-IR spectra. As shown in Fig. S3, the broad absorption band at 3378 cm−1 was assigned to stretching vibrations of –OH. The peak at 1642 cm−1 corresponded to the C=O stretching vibration. The characteristic absorption band at 1120 cm−1 represented stretching vibrations of C-O. The above results provided by XPS and FT-IR analysis substantially suggested the existence of residual hydroxyl groups and other oxygen-containing groups mainly including carbonyl groups produced by the surface oxidation [40,41].

The UV−vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were carried out to explore the optical properties of the as-prepared CDs. As shown in Fig. 3A, in the UV region, the CDs displayed a characteristic absorption peak at 225 nm and a shoulder at 310 nm, corresponding to the π→π* and n→π* electron transition of C=C and of C=O, respectively. Furthermore, the absorption ranging from 450 to 500 nm may originate from the surface states of the CDs [42,43]. The fluorescence property of the CDs was investigated under different excitation wavelengths. As displayed in Fig. 3B, by increasing the excitation wavelength from 380 to 500 nm with interval of 20 nm, the fluorescence emission wavelength gradually red-shifted from 495 to 565 nm. The wide range of photoluminescence and excitation-dependent fluorescence feature might be ascribed to the variation of particle size and different functional groups such as carbonyl and hydroxyl groups present on the surfaces of the CDs, as in the case of most fluorescent CDs [42–44]. The maximum emission intensity for the CDs was achieved at 504 nm when excited at 400 nm. With irradiation by a 365 nm UV lamp, the CDs solution emitted a bright yellow-green fluorescence (inset in Fig. 3B). Furthermore, they exhibited excellent photostability as the fluorescence intensity changed little under stored at 4 °C (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of water using the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It is found that the as-prepared CDs in ethanol, DMSO, DMF, and ACN emitted bright yellow-green fluorescence while little luminescence was observed in water. Thus we explored the feasibility of the CDs as the fluorescent probes for the detection of water contents in organic solvents. Ethanol was here used as the model solvent. By the introduction of a small amount of water, the fluorescence of the CDs solution showed a very significant decrease. More importantly, such response was finished in less than 1 s (Fig. 4A), indicative of an ultrafast detection and the capability for real-time detection of water in organic solvents. Because excess CDs will increase the background signal and the experimental cost, the amount of used CDs was also investigated to obtain optimum analytical performance. As revealed in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence response of the CDs toward water progressively enhanced with the decrease of the CDs in amount while the sensitivity gradually declined when the amount became too small. The best fluorescence response was obtained using 5.1 mg mL−1 CDs. We also studied the pH effect on water detection using the CDs. As displayed in Fig. S5, in acidic and neutral environment (4–8), the decrease values in the emission intensity of the CDs were very similar. When the pH value was higher than 8, the fluorescence of the CDs unexpectedly increased to some extent. For convenience of experimental operation, we thus chose the neutral environment as the optimal detection condition.

Under the optimized condition, the analytical performance of this system for the detection of water content in ethanol was assessed. Fig. 5A displays that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs continuously decreased as the water content increased from 0 to 10% (v/v). A good linear relationship (R2 = 0.995) was obtained between the change of fluorescence intensity at 504 nm (ΔF = F0 F) and water content in the range of 0–1% (Fig. 5B), where F0 and F presented the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of water, respectively. A low limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.006% based on a 3δ/s (δ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and s is the slope of the linear calibration plot). Moreover, with gradual increase of the water content in ethanol, the fluorescence color of the CDs changed from intense yellow-green to colourless easily identified by naked eyes under UV excitation (inset in Fig. 5A), indicating the color change is quite sensitive even when the water content is lower than 10%.

Subsequently, the sensing of CDs toward water (0–10%, v/v) was assessed in other organic solvents such as MeOH, ACN, anhydrous DMSO and DMF. All showed similar fluorescence spectral changes with slightly different sensitivities (Fig. S6-S9). The corresponding LOD were determined to be 0.007% in MeOH, 0.007% in ACN, 0.008% in anhydrous DMSO, and 0.011% in DMF (Table S1). Therefore, the CDs we prepared can be employed as a general agent for fluorescence detection of water content in various organic solvents. We then evaluated the selectivity of this system to water by testing the fluorescence response toward other organic solvents. Remarkably, no apparent fluorescence signal change of the CD solutions was observed (Fig. 6 A). These results clearly demonstrate that the CDs-based water sensor is highly selective against other common organic solvents.

To test the reusability of this CDs-based sensor for water, DCC was used as a dehydration agent to recover the quenched fluorescence of the CDs by water. As shown in Fig. 6B, the bright fluorescence of the CDs was strongly quenched to 47.31% of the initial intensity after addition of small quantities of water (10%, v/v), while it can be recovered to almost the initial intensity by introducing the same amount of DCC. After five cycles, the fluorescence switch still showed excellent reversibility. Such good reversibility is mainly ascribed to the stoichiometric addition reaction between water and DCC and reversible surface states of the CDs [30]. These observations revealed that the detection of water based on the CDs can operate in recyclable way thus offering a significant advantage over organic molecule-based sensors.

Compared with recently reported fluorescent sensors for water based on inorganic nanomaterials, our developed the CDs-based sensor shows some distinguished advantages as listed in Table S2. First, the CDs were readily prepared by one step using only one precursor without any postmodification or activation process, which is technically simple and cost-effective. Second, this assay showed excellent analytical performance including high sensitivity and selectivity, as well as good and easy-to-operate reusability. Especially, unlike most porous materials where water molecules are adsorbed into their pore structure after minutes to hours or even longer, the present probe's response is instantaneous. Third, the CDs we synthesized are suitable for the detection of water content in polar protic solvents like ethanol, demonstrating little influence from the strong polarity and hydrogen bonding from the sensing media usually occurred for organic molecule-based sensors. These features essentially make the CDs very promising in practical application to water detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the fluorescence response of the CDs to H2O
ce:section.ce:para: As described above, the trace amount of water could strongly quench the fluorescence of the CDs in this study. Previous reports demonstrate that the emission properties of the CDs may primarily be influenced by the particle size and surface states [42,43]. In order to explore the water detection mechanism, we first did the TEM experiment on the CDs after adding water. As shown in Fig. S10, it verified that the size of the CDs scarcely changed, which excluded the possibility of aggregation-caused quenching observed in many reports about luminescent inorganic nanomaterials. Moreover, a XRD pattern similar to that of pure CDs was obtained after adding water (Fig. S2), indicating good structural integrity. So, we tried to correlate the change of surface states with such quenching phenomenon. This change was tested by XPS analysis for the CDs after the addition of water. As shown in Fig. 7 A, the XPS survey spectrum showed a C 1s peak at ca. 298.1 eV, along with an O 1s peak at ca. 545.1 eV. And the O/C atomic ratio increased to 0.34 upon contact with water, which indicated that water molecules were decorated onto the CDs. Furthermore, the high resolution O 1s XPS spectrum indicates only one peak (Fig. 7B), which corresponded to the signal of C-O (533.1 eV). Nevertheless, that of C 1s can be divided into two different peaks located at 284.8 eV and 286.3 eV (Fig. 7C), which were assigned to the signals of C-C/C=C and C-O, respectively. These two high resolution XPS spectra both suggested the disappearance of C=O groups compared to those of pure CDs. A similar result was also found for the IR spectrum of the CDs in the presence of water. As shown in Fig. S3, after adding water, the peak corresponding to the C=O stretching vibration weakened greatly while the characteristic absorption band assigned to C-O stretching vibration became much stronger than before; meanwhile, the -OH absorption bands became stronger and broader. Thus we speculate that water molecules reacted with the surfaces of the CDs through the hydration of the carbonyl groups to form a gem-diol species, leading to the increase of the density of C–OH group accordingly (Scheme 1) [8,17]. The zeta potentials of the CDs before and after adding water showed a very small change (Fig. S11), which could be elucidated by the decrease in the amount of negatively-charged carbonyl groups by producing hydroxyl groups in the presence of water as speculated.

It is reported that the variation in surface functional groups of the CDs can result in the change in UV−vis absorption spectrum [42,43], so we further investigated the change of the absorption spectrum of the CDs caused by water. As shown in Fig. S12, as a small amount of water was successively added, the absorption band between 450 and 500 nm decreased gradually accompanied with a slow color fading, which strongly supported the decrement of the surface oxidation states of the CDs because such a band was ascribed to n → π* electron transition for peripheric C=O groups [42]. Additionally, the emission position had a blue shift to some extent accompanied by the attenuation of the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 5 and S6-S9), which also proved less surface oxidation states [42,43].

Other experiments were applied to gain more insight to the fluorescence quenching mechanism. As depicted in Fig. 7D, after the addition of water, the lifetime of the CDs decreased to 3.80 ns, which was shorter than pure CDs (4.72 ns). Similarly, the QY of the CDs in the presence of water (0.99%) became much lower than in the absence of water (6.9%). In general, QY = Γ/(Γ+ k nr), where Γ and k nr are radiative and nonradiative decay rate, respectively, and τ = 1/(Γ+ k nr), where τ represents the fluorescence lifetime [45]. So it can be inferred that the presence of water greatly promoted the nonradiative decay pathways in the CDs.

Based on these experimental results, it could be concluded that the water-induced fluorescence quenching was presumably because the surface states, mainly carbonyl groups, on the surfaces of the CDs converted to the hydrate in the presence of water. The surfacial carbonyl groups would bring surface defects to the CDs, which are considered to contribute to the fluorescence by trapping the excitons [42,43]. Therefore, the formation of the hydrate of the CDs reduced the degree of surface oxidation, consequently leading to fluorescence quenching by nonradiative electron-transfer owing to less recombination process of trapped excitons.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of water content in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to validate the practical application, the fluorescence assay was applied to detect water content in various spirit samples with the CDs. The each sample was assayed and the water contents were calculated using the calibration equation in Fig. 5B. Table S3 showed that the results were comparable to those obtained by the trademark, indicating great potential of the CDs-based fluorescence probe for detecting water in spirit samples.

To detect water content in various spirit samples simply and quickly, the fluorescence paper test strips were prepared by immersed into the CDs solution and subsequent drying. As revealed in Fig. 8 , after depositing a drop of the samples onto the paper strips, the fluorescence color of the strips displayed different depths from intense yellow-green to almost colorless, suggesting different water contents in the samples. This makes the sensor quite useful for immediate on-site qualitative detection of the water content in real samples.

In summary, we developed a new, low-cost, and high-efficient method for the preparation of the CDs with a simple one-pot route. The CDs emitting strong yellow-green fluorescence were used as a potent fluorescent probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of water in various organic solvents. It was demonstrated that the introduction of water could modulate the CDs surface oxidation-related fluorescence through the hydration of the carbonyl groups. Moreover, the obtained CDs showed ultrafast response and favorable reversibility. Finally, the fluorescence assay was successfully applied to the detection of water in spirit samples, which holds the promise in the detection of water with the CDs in the quality control and production processes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We greatly acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21765014 and 21505067) and the Opening Project of Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine, South China Normal University, Guangzhou, China (No. 2018001) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is/are the supplementary data related to this article:
Supplementary lnformation Supplementary lnformation

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.06.003

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Humidity-sensitive optode membrane based on a fluorescent dye immobilized in gelatin film
A new method for the analytical determination of the water content of liquids and solids
Automation of Karl Fischer water titration by flow injection sampling
Water determination in solid pharmaceutical products utilizing ionic liquids and headspace gas chromatography
Detection of very low humidity using polyelectrolyte/graphene bilayer humidity sensors
Fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for the detection of humidity or water content
Highly sensitive fluorescent sensing for water based on poly(m-aminobenzoic acid)
A water indicator strip: instantaneous fluorogenic detection of water in organic solvents, drugs, and foodstuffs
A dual channel optical detector for trace water chemodosimetry and imaging of live cells
Spectroscopic response of ferrocene derivatives bearing a BODIPY moiety to water: a new dissociation reaction
Highly sensitive determination of low-level water content in organic solvents using novel solvatochromic dyes based on thioxanthone
Highly sensitive detection of low-level water content in organic solvents and cyanide in aqueous media using novel solvatochromic AIEE fluorophores
A highly efficient turn-on fluorescent sensor for determination of water in organic solvents
A simple multi-responsive system based on aldehyde functionalized amino-boranes
Signaling of water content in organic solvents by solvatochromism of a hydroxynaphthalimide-based merocyanine dye
Ultrasensitive 4-methylumbelliferone fluorimetric determination of water contents in aprotic solvents
Water detection by “turn on” fluorescence of the quinone-containing complexes [Ru(phen)2(1,10-phenanthroline-5,6-dione)2+] and [Ru(phenanthroline-5,6-dione)3]2+
Design and synthesis of a library of BODIPY-based environmental polarity sensors utilizing photoinduced electron-transfer-controlled fluorescence on/off switching
PET-dependent fluorescence sensing of enzyme reactions using the large and tunable pKa shift of aliphatic amines
Turn-on luminescence sensing and real-time detection of traces of water in organic solvents by a flexible metal-organic framework
Ultrafast water sensing and thermal imaging by a metal-organic framework with switchable luminescence
Highly selective chemical sensing in a luminescent nanoporous magnet
